rulings.Case Title: JANET TAPIA VS. WESTERN ADDITION CENTER ET AL
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, AUGUST 10, 2018, LINE 8 PLAINTIFF JANET TAPIA'S Motion For A New Trial DENIED. The court lacks jurisdiction to grant a new trial. First, Tapia failed to file her notice of intention to move for a new trial in a timely fashion. The notice of entry of judgment was mail-served on May 17, 2018 and Tapia filed her notice of intention on July 5, 2018. This filing was outside the jurisdictional 15-day time period of CCP § 659(a)(2). (See In re Marriage of Beilock (1978) 81 Cal.App.3d 713, 721.) In addition, the court lacks jurisdiction to grant a new trial because the 60-day period to rule ran on July 16, 2018. (See CCP § 660.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = RBU

rulings.Case Title: CRAIG POWERS ET AL VS. DECLAN ROGERS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Soren Lassen And Sharmila Lassen'S Motion To Compel Plainitff'S Further Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set One, And Request For Fees, Costs And Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff And His Attorney Of Record
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, August 10, 2018, Line 2, Defendants Soren Lassen And Sharmila Lassen'S Motion To Compel Plainitff'S Further Responses To Special Interrogatories, Set One, And Request For Fees, Costs And Monetary Sanctions Against Plaintiff And His Attorney Of Record. Pro Tem Judge Tom Cohen, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Continued to August 14, 2018 at 9:00 am per agreement of the parties and approval of the Court. (email dated 8/8/18) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tom@thomascohen.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DAVID MOURRA ET AL VS. ROMAN SHTYLMAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Roman Shtylman And Bitfloor, Inc.'S Motion For Relief From Order Pursuant To C.C.P. Section 473
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, AUGUST 10, 2018, LINE 9 Defendants Roman Shtylman And Bitfloor, Inc.'S Motion For Relief From Order Pursuant To C.C.P. Section 473 DENIED. Back in June, I overruled defendants' demurrer asserting lack of personal jurisdiction, because it was contrary to California law. "Lack of personal jurisdiction over defendant is not ground for demurrer. It must be raised, if at all, by motion to quash service of summons [CCP §418.10¿] If defendant instead demurs, the demurrer constitutes a general appearance, waiving any defect in personal jurisdiction [CCP §1014]". (Weil & Brown, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) p. 7(I)-36 (emphasis in original); see also id at p. 3-106.) Defendants now move for relief under Code of Civil Procedure §473, conceding their "mistake," but claiming it was "excusable." However, defendants also concede that whether a mistake is excusable is judged by whether a "reasonably prudent" attorney would have made it. (See Zamora v. Clayborn Contracting Group, Inc. (2002) 28 Cal.4th 249, 258.) Reasonably prudent California litigation lawyers know the requirements for challenging personal jurisdiction. Even attorneys who do not know the rules can ascertain them in a matter of seconds - e.g., by consulting the Weil & Brown treatise. Moreover, defendants cite no authority holding that, once waived, a lack of personal jurisdiction claim can be revived. Defendants say their New York lawyers drafted the demurrers, but pro hac vice counsel are subject to the same professional standards as California counsel. (See Weil & Brown, p. 9(I)-168; CRC 9.40.) Defendants' motion is DENIED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = RBU

rulings.Case Title: GOLDEN GATEWAY CENTER VS. PANKOW SPECIAL PROJECTS, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON FRIDAY, AUGUST 10, 2018, LINE 4, Motion 1 DEFENDANTS ROYSTON, HANAMOTO, ALLEY & ABEY'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT OVERRULED as to the first and third causes of action for breach of contract and negligence and SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND as to the second cause of action for breach of warranty. Liberally construed, the complaint's factual allegations do not show that the defects were obvious ones discoverable upon a reasonable inspection, nor was plaintiff required to hire an expert to investigate for construction defects upon the occurrence of minor maintenance problems. (See Creekridge Townhome Owners Assn., Inc. v. C. Scott Whitten, Inc. (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 251, 256-59; Geertz v. Ausonio (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 1363, 1371.) Plaintiff has sufficiently pled the time and manner of discovery of the alleged latent defects (FAC 15, 17-20) and the reason for the late discovery has also been alleged (id. at 13-15). Thus, on its face, the complaint is not time-barred. While claims for implied warranties against design professionals are not proper, claims for express warranties based on a written or oral contract can be maintained. (Gagne v. Bertran (1954) 43 Cal.2d 481, 487.) The written contract language relied upon against RHAA cannot be fairly read to provide an express warranty, because the promises that RHAA would visit the site and reject non-conforming work does not guarantee a particular result. Plaintiff is given leave to sufficiently allege the oral contract on which its express warranty claim is based. (See Stevenson v. San Francisco Housing Authority (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 269, 284.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Golden Gateway is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. = RBU

Liberally construed, the complaint's factual allegations do not show that the defects were obvious ones discoverable upon a reasonable inspection, nor was plaintiff required to hire an expert to investigate for construction defects upon the occurrence of minor maintenance problems. (Creekridge Townhome Owners Assn., Inc. v. C. Scott Whitten, Inc. (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 251, 256-59; Geertz v. Ausonio (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 1363, 1371.) Plaintiff has sufficiently pled the time and manner of the discovery of the alleged latent defects (FAC 15, 17-20) and the reason for the late discovery has also been alleged (id. at 13-15). Thus, on its face, the complaint is not time-barred. While claims for implied warranties against professionals such a defendants are not proper, claims for express warranties based on a written or oral contract can be maintained. (Gagne v. Bertran (1954) 43 Cal.2d 481, 487.) Plaintiff has sufficiently alleged an express warranty based on a written contract. (FAC 37.)
